critic_raw:
This experiment comparing a hybrid CNN-RNN against logistic regression for hospital readmission prediction demonstrates solid attention to data leakage prevention, temporal splitting, and preprocessing discipline. However, there are important methodological concerns that undermine the fairness and interpretability of the comparison.

The most significant issue is that the hybrid CNN-RNN and logistic regression models receive fundamentally different input representations: the CNN-RNN processes raw temporal sequences of treatment events, while logistic regression uses engineered summary features. This creates a dual confound between model architecture and feature representation. If the CNN-RNN outperforms logistic regression, the advantage could be attributable to the richer sequential input rather than the model's architectural capacity. A proper ablation would test logistic regression on the same raw or sequential features (e.g., using bag-of-events encodings or temporal aggregations) to isolate architectural contribution.

Department-level stratification within temporal windows is applied to address department-specific confounds, but the use of department-level readmission baseline rates as features in both models introduces a subtle data construction dependency: these rates are computed from the training set and included as features, but their informativeness will differ between the complex CNN-RNN (which can learn non-linear interactions) and logistic regression (which uses them linearly). This asymmetry could systematically favor one model.

The decision to record the validation set AUPRC as the primary model selection criterion, while AUPRC is also a secondary evaluation metric reported on the test set, requires more care. If AUPRC on validation is used for selection and then reported as a secondary metric on test alongside AUROC, the test AUPRC will be upwardly biased relative to a model selected on AUROC. This selection metric alignment issue is not discussed.

The exclusion criterion (records with >20% missing values in key fields) is stated but the threshold of 20% is arbitrary and not validated. Systematic missingness patterns (e.g., sicker patients have more incomplete records) could introduce selection bias that differentially affects the model comparison, particularly if the CNN-RNN is more sensitive to missingness patterns in sequences.
all_issues_raised: CNN-RNN and logistic regression receive different input representations (raw sequences vs. engineered features), confounding architecture vs. feature richness as the source of any performance gap
Department-level baseline rates as features create an asymmetric advantage that interacts differently with non-linear vs. linear models
Validation metric (AUPRC) alignment with test secondary metrics introduces potential upward bias in reported test AUPRC
20% missingness exclusion threshold is arbitrary; systematic missingness patterns could introduce selection bias that differentially impacts models
No ablation separating sequential modeling from richer feature access; logistic regression is not tested on equivalent sequential or bag-of-events features
Logistic regression baseline lacks sequence-informed features, conflating architectural difference with feature richness rather than isolating architectural contribution
Stratification by hospital department does not guarantee class balance across temporal splits; readmission rate comparability across splits is unverified
AUPRC as primary metric is sensitive to class prevalence drift across temporal windows, making cross-split comparisons potentially misleading
CNN architecture on medication/procedure sequences lacks theoretical justification — CNNs assume local translational invariance not present in clinical event sequences
No ablation of CNN-only versus RNN-only versus full CNN-RNN to identify which architectural component drives any observed performance gains
CNN-RNN receives raw sequence data while logistic regression receives aggregate features, confounding architectural comparison with information asymmetry
Fixed CNN architecture (2 blocks, specific filter counts) may reflect implicit pre-study choices not captured in the formal hyperparameter search, introducing researcher degrees of freedom
No statistical significance testing for AUPRC differences on the test set; results from a single temporal slice may be within sampling noise
Department-level readmission baseline features derived from training data may become stale or misleading if department case-mix shifts during the test period
Single hospital system limits external validity; no discussion of whether temporal distribution shift across the 5-year window was assessed prior to modeling
